coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent turn on sensing of Caffeine in food sample based on sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots and optimization of process parameters through response surface methodology
coredata.dc:creator: Nemati, Fatemeh
Hosseini, Morteza
Zare-Dorabei, Rouholah
Salehnia, Foad
Ganjali, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: We have demonstrated a fluorescence approach for rapid, simple and selective detection of caffeine in real media using sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) as probe. S-CQDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted treatment using citric acid as carbon source and sodium thiosulfate as passivator, respectively. The obtained S-CQDs emitted strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light excitation with a high quantum yield of 21.4%. The fluorescence of S-CQDs is strongly quenched by Cu2+ mainly due to dynamic quenching. A detailed investigation of the quenching mechanisms was carried out. Upon addition of caffeine, the fluorescence of S-CQDs was recovered due to the formation of copper-caffeine complexes. Therefore, we designed an off-on fluorescent probe for detection of caffeine based on this phenomenon. The effective parameters in sensing process, including time, temperature, pH and concentration of S-CQDs, were optimized using response surface methodology (RSM) based on central composite design. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the system increased linearly with increasing concentrations of caffeine from 0.2 μM to 70 μM. The limit of detection of caffeine was 0.05 μM. The established method showed a high selectivity for caffeine activity compared with other interference components. Finally, the carbon quantum dots −based fluorescent probe was successfully employed for the caffeine detection in real sample, and satisfactory results were achieved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Turn-on sensor
Food samples
Caffeine
Microwave
Experimental design

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing principles of caffeine based on S-CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Iran University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the research Council of University of Tehran (Grant 28645/01/02 ) and Iran University of Science and Technology for financial support of this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation process of S-CQDs from citric acid and sodium thiosulfate by microwave synthesis and the Cu2+ and caffeine detection mechanism using G-CDs.
(A) TEM image, (B) size distribution, (C) FTIR spectrum, (D) EDS spectra and (E) XRD patterns of obtained S-CQD.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the obtained S-CQDs. Inset: photographs of S-CQDs in aqueous solutions under visible light (yellow) and under UV lamp (blue). (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of S-CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) Predicted vs. experimental values (B) run number vs. internally studentized residual (C) normal probability plot of residuals.
Response surfaces for the central composite designs: (A) temperature-pH (B) time- temperature (C) [S-CQDs]-temperature (D) [S-CQDs]- pH (E) [S-CQDs]- time (F) pH- time.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of S-CQDs with different concentration of Cu2+. (B) The dependence of (F0/F) (where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively) on the concentrations of added Cu2+.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of S-CQDs containing Cu2+with different concentration of caffeine (B) Relationship between [(F − F0)/F0] and the concentration of caffeine.

(A) The fluorescence enhancement factor [(F-F0)/F0] responses to caffeine over various interfering compounds. (B) The competitive selectivity experiments with the presence interfering species. Black bars represent the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs- Cu2+ containing caffeine in the absence of each interferents. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent measurements.
Comparison of the presented method with other approaches for the caffeine detection.
Analytical results of caffeine in food samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescent turn on sensing of Caffeine in food sample based on sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots and optimization of process parameters through response surface methodology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Spectrometry & Micro and Nano Extraction, Department of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Spectrometry & Micro and Nano Extraction
Iran University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Biomaterials and Medical Biomaterials Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Biomaterials and Medical Biomaterials Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence in Electrochemistry, School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biosensor Research Center, Endocrinology and Metabolism Molecular-Cellular Sciences Institute, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Endocrinology and Metabolism Molecular-Cellular Sciences Institute

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have demonstrated a fluorescence approach for rapid, simple and selective detection of caffeine in real media using sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) as probe. S-CQDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted treatment using citric acid as carbon source and sodium thiosulfate as passivator, respectively. The obtained S-CQDs emitted strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light excitation with a high quantum yield of 21.4%. The fluorescence of S-CQDs is strongly quenched by Cu2+ mainly due to dynamic quenching. A detailed investigation of the quenching mechanisms was carried out. Upon addition of caffeine, the fluorescence of S-CQDs was recovered due to the formation of copper-caffeine complexes. Therefore, we designed an off-on fluorescent probe for detection of caffeine based on this phenomenon. The effective parameters in sensing process, including time, temperature, pH and concentration of S-CQDs, were optimized using response surface methodology (RSM) based on central composite design. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the system increased linearly with increasing concentrations of caffeine from 0.2 μM to 70 μM. The limit of detection of caffeine was 0.05 μM. The established method showed a high selectivity for caffeine activity compared with other interference components. Finally, the carbon quantum dots −based fluorescent probe was successfully employed for the caffeine detection in real sample, and satisfactory results were achieved.

ce:section.ce:para: Caffeine is an active alkaloid component belonging to xanthine family that is found in many natural products [1,2]. It is most commonly consumed in coffee, tea and beverages (such as cola, soft and energy drinks) [3]. Pure caffeine is white odorless powder and has a bitter taste [4]. In plants, caffeine acts as a natural pesticide, where it paralyzes and kills some insects that try to feed on the plant [5]. Caffeine in human has many physiological effects including gastric acid secretion, diuresis and stimulation of both heart and central nervous system [6]. After its oral intake, caffeine is quickly absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract [7] and distributed into total body fluids and tissues [8,9]. It is noteworthy that consumption of high amounts of caffeine can cause undesired effects such as headache, gastrointestinal problem, anxiety and tremors [10]. Due to its common use and its effects on human health, the development of a rapid, simple, reliable and sensitive method for monitoring caffeine is thus required. Many analytical methods, including high performance liquid chromatography [11], voltammetry [12], capillary electrophoresis [13], micellar liquid chromatography [14] and ion mobility spectrometry [15] have been proposed for the determination of caffeine in various matrices. However, these techniques are often time-consuming and require very expensive equipment and highly skilled technicians. Compared to these conventional analytical techniques, fluorescence sensing with suitable probes has become an efficient method in analytical fields due to its simplicity, high sensitivity, and easy performances [16–22]. Over the last decade, quantum dots (QDs) has received considerable attention for sensing applications because of high photoluminescence yields, size- tunable light emission and good resistance to photobleaching [23,24]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently emerged as a new kind of carbon nanomaterial with a size of less than 10 nm with wide applications such as drug delivery [25], photo-catalysis [26], energy devices [27]. CQDs have also attracted great attention from researchers as sensor materials compared to traditional metal quantum dots and organic dyes due to their attractive properties including convenient surface functionalization, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity and high aqueous solubility [28,29]. However, in most cases, the prepared CQDs exhibit a low quantum yield (QY), which limits the number of their practical applications [30]. It has been shown that doping carbon quantum dots with heteroatoms is an effective way to modify their optical properties [31]. Element doping can penetrate into the carbon lattice of CQDs and introduce new energy levels or change their band gap [32,33].The performance of sensing can be significantly affected by different parameters. Because traditional single variable optimization approach cannot examine the combined effect of factors, response surface methodology (RSM) was utilized to determine the variables that significantly affect the sensor efficiency. RSM is mainly a collaboration of mathematical and statistical tools applied for experimental design, process optimization and mathematical model generation [34]. In this approach, the effect of process parameters, alone or in combination, is represented through predictive models in conjunction with ANOVA (Analysis Of Variance) [35]. In this work, we present a fluorescent sensor system for sensitive and rapid detection of caffeine in food samples by use of the sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots, which is prepared by a facile microwave-assisted method (Scheme 1 ). Based on the excellent photoluminescence of the obtained S-CQDs, a simple, potent, low cost and Eco-friendly fluorescent probe was designed. The effect of various parameters on the fluorescence sensing process were studied and optimized by RSM technique based on central composite design (CCD). The optimized procedure was utilized to analysis of caffeine in food samples.

Citric acid (+99%), sodium thiosulfate and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich Co. Stock standard solution of Caffeine (1.0 × 10−3 mol L−1) was prepared by dissolving the corresponding amount of Caffeine (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) in double distilled water. Working solutions of lower concentrations were daily prepared by suitable dilutions of the stock solution with double distilled water. All chemicals were used with analytical grade. Deionized water (resistivity greater than 18 MΩ) produced by a Millipore Milli-Q system was used in all experiments.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) adsorption spectra were measured in the range of 200 nm to 700 nm on a T80 spectrophotometer (PG Instruments). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer LS50 luminescence spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (400–4000 cm−1) in KBr was obtained using a Shimadzu-8400S spectrometer. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis was performed using a scanning electron microscope (Model XL30 ESEM, Philips). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on Philips CM30 microscope. The pH adjustments and measurements were performed by pH meter Metrohm. Zeta potential was performed using the zetasizer (Marvalen, Model of nano-zs). The X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were obtained using a Phillips X'pert MPD diffractometer.

The S-CQDs were rapidly synthesized via a microwave-assisted method based on the previous work with small modification [36]. Briefly, 0.8 g of sodium thiosulfate and 2.0 g of citric acid were mixed with deionized water (5 mL). Then, the clear solution was heated in a 700- W household microwave oven for 5 min. The resulting material was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles. Subsequently, the resultant supernatant was filtered with a cylinder membrane filter (0.22 μm) and the resulting suspension containing S-CQDs was collected for further characterization and use.

For caffeine detection, S-CQDs solution with specific concentration (0.07 mg/ml), selected based on the experimental design result, was added to 1 mL phosphate buffer (pH = 7) and together with 40 μL (0.01 M) of Cu2+ solution were transferred to a fluorescence cuvette and then mixed well. Afterward, the above mixture was diluted with deionized water to get a final volume of 2.00 mL. Then, various amounts of Caffeine were added to the cuvette. Ninety seconds later, fluorescence spectra of the mixed solutions were measured at λex = 340 nm and the slit widths of 10.0 and 15.0 nm for excitation and emission, respectively.

We determined the quantum yield of the as-prepared S-CQDs by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY = 0.54 in water) as the standard sample. The quantum yields of fluorescent substances were calculated based on the below equation (Eq. (1)):
(1)
Y u
=
Y
s t
I u
I
s t
A
s t
A u
n u 2
n
s t
2
Where Y is the quantum yield, I corresponds to the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index for the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript “st” refers to the quinine sulfate with known QY and “u” for sample.

Three types of tea (black, green and earl grey black tea bag), two kinds of coffee cappuccino (Klassno, Singapore) and coffee (Nescafe, Brazil), and two different beverages (Coca Cola and Hype) were purchased from a local supermarket in Tehran city, Iran. Following steps were carried out for the fluorescent recognition of caffeine in real samples: 0.5 g of tea (dried, ground and sieved) was transferred into the beaker. 50 mL of distilled boiled water was added and stayed for 15 min. The mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to remove the solid particles from the solution. In the case of coffee samples, 1 g of powdered sample was added to 100 mL of distilled water and heated to boiling. Then, the solutions were filtered using a 0.45 μm membrane to obtain a clear solution. Beverage samples were degassed prior to application of the method for 15 min using an ultrasonic bath. Afterwards, the solutions were filtered using a cellulose membrane filter of 0.45 μm pore size.

In the present work, dependency of enhancement ratio (F-F0/F0) to four variables viz. pH, incubation time, temperature and concentration of S-CQDs was examined. We used a central composite design (CCD) as a simultaneous optimization method to investigate the optimization conditions, interaction and main effect of studied parameters. A central composite design, in response surface methodology, is the most commonly used experimental design for modeling and optimization [37]. An initial screening study based on the literature review was performed to select the appropriate ranges for selected variables. The factors were set in five-level, which designed 22 (Six replicates for center point and 16 for not centers) experimental runs. The coded values of levels were represented in Table S1. The relation between the four independent variables was approximated by the second order polynomial model:
(2)
Y =
β
0
+
∑
i = 1
k
β i
x i
+
∑
i = 1
k
∑
j = i + 1
k
β
i j
x i
x j
+
∑
i = 1
k
β
i i
x i
2
In this equation, x i and x j indicate the coded variables; β 0 is constant for the model; βi is linear coefficient. The coefficient of quadratic effect is βii, and the coefficient of interactive effect is βij. The analysis of variance (ANOVA) was implemented to assess the statistical significance of each factor, their interactions and adequacy of the developed regression model. The statistical software Design − Expert 7.0 was used in order to evaluate the results and experimental design. The experimentally obtained response from 22 runs are displayed in Table S2.

TEM images of the synthesized S-CQDs were displayed in Fig. 1 A. The result showed that the prepared CQD were well dispersed and distributed within the range of 3.0–9 nm (Fig. 1B). The FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. 1C were used to recognize the surface functional groups of the synthesized S-CQDs. The broad absorption bands at 3430 cm−1 was attributed to OH stretching vibration. The peaks at 1708 and 2904 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of CO and CH. Peak at 635 cm−1 for CS/CSC bending and the peak at 1040 cm−1 were belonged to CO stretching vibrations [36]. Structural properties of S-CQDs were characterized by an X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis. Fig. 1D shows a broad peak between 19 and 24.33°, with sharp peaks at around 25.65 and 27.57°. It can be concluded that the prepared S-CQDs contain both crystallines and amorphous phases of carbon [38,39]. Energy-dispersive analysis of X-rays (EDAX) was used to investigate the composition of the synthesized S-CQDs. EDAX spectra of the S-CQDs showed the presence of carbon, sulfur and oxygen (Fig. 1E). The TEM image and TEM-energy-dispersive X-ray (TEM-EDX) elemental mapping showed overall uniform distribution of C, S, and O in the sample (Fig. S1). These results confirmed the successful S-doping via the microwave-assisted method. Optical properties of the resulting S-CQDs were evaluated by fluorescence and UV–vis techniques. The UV–vis spectrum of the S-CQDs (Fig. 2 A) shows two typical absorption peaks at 213 and 346 nm, which are corresponded to π → π* transition of CC and n → π* transition of CO, respectively. The light yellow S-CQDs aqueous solution exhibited strong blue-green fluorescence emission upon excitation by a 365 nm lamp (Fig. 2A inset). The emission spectra in Fig. 2B showed that a strong emission with the maximum emission wavelength at 435 nm could be observed under excitation at 340 nm (Fig. 2B). Additionally, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the obtained S-CQDs was about 21.4% with quinine sulfate as the calculated reference standard. Moreover, the emission peaks of the present S-CQDs depend strongly on the excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. S2, the peak intensity decreased with increasing excitation wavelength from 330 nm to 410 nm and the peak wavelengths were shifted to a higher wavelength. Therefore, all experiments were done with the excitation wavelength at 340 nm.

The stability of S-CQDs as a probe plays the key role in their practical applications. The photostability of S-CQDs was evaluated after UV irradiation for 100 min. As can be seen in Fig. S3, the fluorescence signal of S-CQDs stayed stable within the studied time, indicating an excellent photostability. The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs was also explored in the study. Fig. S4 shows that varying NaCl concentrations from 0.1 to 0.8 M had little effect on the fluorescence signal of S-CQDs. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs did not show significant change after 60 days storage (Fig. S5).The fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs was studied under different pH (2–10) as shown in Fig S6. It could be observed that the S-CQDs showed stable fluorescence character over a wide range of pH values; 4.0–9.0.

Prior to quantitative analysis of caffeine using the suggested probe, we employed a central composite design for optimization of detection conditions, including the pH value, concentration of S-CQDs used in the proposed sensor, incubation time and temperature of the system. The experimentally obtained response values are showed in Table 2. Based on the quadratic polynomial equation model Eq. (2) and the experimental data, the relationship between the response (F − F0/F0) and process parameters could be demonstrated by Eq. (3)
(3) F − F0/F0 = +1.67 − 0.060x 1 + 0.065x 2 − 0.097x 3 − 0.017x 4 − 0.012x 1 x 2 + 0.083x 1 x 3 + 0.062x 1 x 4 + 0.018x 2 x 3 + 0.022x 2 x 4 + 0.035x 3 x 4 − 0.33x 1 2 − 0.16x 2 2 − 0.079x 3 2 − 0.060x 4 2
Where x1, x2, x3 and x4 are pH, time, temperature and S-CQDs concentration, respectively. As mentioned before, the analysis of variance (a collection of statistical models) makes it possible to identify the effect of important factors and their possible interactions during the empirical analysis. The quality of fit of the proposed model was judged statistically by the coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.9876 and adjusted-R2 = 0.9586). The R2 and adjusted R2 values indicate magnificent relationship between experimental data and empirical model which also show a high efficiency applied model for well prediction of response. According to the data obtained from ANOVA, the amounts of F-value (34.11) and low p-value (<0.0001) indicates that the constructed model is significant. Lack of fit (LOF) parameter shows the variation of data around the fitted model. If the model fit the data well, this parameter will not be significant. The lack-of-fit (LOF) P-value of 0.5907(more than 0.05 critical value) indicates that the LOF was not significant relative to the pure error. Fig. 3 A represents the relationship between model predicted values against actual data, As observed, actual data were distributed in close proximity of the straight line, which indicates the accuracy and robustness of model. In addition, the plot of studentized residuals versus run number was checked (Fig. 3B). The random pattern of residuals shows high efficiency of model for appropriate presentation of actual data. The normal probability plot (Fig. 3C) shows that the points followed a normal distribution and approximated along a straight line [40]. Fig. 4 illustrates the effect of interaction between independent variables on enrichment ratio as three-dimensional surface. In these plots, one factor was kept constant while the others were varied within the experimental ranges. On the response surfaces for the effect of temperature (Fig. 4A–C) it can be seen that the enhancement ratio declines with increasing in initial temperature of solution. This behavior can be understood considering that the quantum efficiency of photon excitation involves both radiative decay and non-radiative decay. Normally, the radiative decay rate is not influenced by temperature but non-radiative decay rate is temperature dependent by the Boltzmann distribution as: A ⋅−exp(-E/kT), where T is the thermodynamic temperature, k is the Boltzmann's constant, E is state energy and A is a constant. With increasing temperature, the non-radiative decay rate increases and causes decreased quantum efficiency and corresponding photoluminescence intensity [41]. The response surface plots of Fig. 4C–E demonstrates a significant decrease of the enhancement ratio, by increasing concentration of S-CQDs. As concentration increases, self-quenching can occur since the collision frequency will be high. Therefore, the high concentration of S-CQDs leads to a decrease in the initial intensity [42]. For this reason, concentration of S-CQD was determined to be 0.07 mg/ml for the further caffeine sensing. The effects of pH in the range of 3–11 (Fig. 4A, D, and F) reveal that the best response was obtained in pH = 7. Under alkaline conditions, Cu2+ reacts with the OH− in solution to form Cu(OH)2 and the quenching effect of the Cu2+ would be weakened. On the other hand, in acidic conditions, the CQDs self-assemble into larger particles through hydrogen bonds between surface oxygen-related groups of CQDs. Additionally, at acidic pH, the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of S-CQDs can be protonated [43,44]. Fig. 4B, E and F depicts the effect of incubation time on the response values. Overall, the enhancement ratio can reach its maximum value within 90 s of contact time, indicating the rapid response of probe to the caffeine which confirm the suitability and efficiency of the proposed system. The optimum parameters of sensing process were determined as time 90s, temperature 30 ∘C, pH = 7 and S-CQDs concentration = 0.07 mg/ml. At this condition, maximum enhancement ratio 1.72 was obtained by the RSM model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing principles of caffeine based on S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
As it can be seen in Fig. 5 A, by increasing the Cu2+ concentrations, the fluorescence of S-CQDs decreases. Additionally, the fluorescence signal of S-CQDs shows no observable change for Cu2+ concentrations of more than 200 μM. Thus, 200 μM was selected as the concentration of Cu2+ in the following experiments. We analyzed the quenching behavior of Cu2+ (F0/F against the Cu2+concentration) by using Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (4)):
(4)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
[ C ]
Where Ksv is quenching constant, C represents the concentration of quencher, Cu2+ in this work; F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of S-CQDs in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively. In our system, the Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 5B) in the range of 0–200 μM shows a linear response (R2 = 0.9827) and the calibration equation was F0/F = 1.09 + 0.0207 [Cu2+]. Dynamic and static quenching effect could not be simply distinguished through Stern-Volmer because both of cases fit into this equation. Fluorescence lifetime measurements can be used to discriminate between these two mechanisms quenching, since in static quenching the fluorescence lifetime of fluorophore is almost constant as opposed to that of dynamic quenching [45]. Therefore, time-resolved fluorescence spectra of S-CQDs, S-CQDs −Cu2+ were measured to understand the mechanism of quenching. From Fig. S5, it could be seen that the data were well fitted with biexponential equation having a short (τ1) and a long (τ2) lifetime components, and the average decay time was given by the sum of the ∑biτi products [46]. As shown in Fig. S7, S-CQDs exhibited two fluorescence decay components, τ1 1.05 ns (63.45%) and τ2 4.47 ns (36.55%). The corresponding average fluorescence time of S-CQDs was about 2.30 ns. When Cu2+ was present, two fluorescence decay components of S-CQDs were shortened to τ1 0.78 ns (72.69.28%) and τ2 3.49 ns (27.31%). The average decay time of S-CQDs was also decreased to around 1.52 ns. This result confirms that fluorescence response of S-CQDs to Cu2+ belongs to the dynamic quenching. Cu2+, a paramagnetic ion with an unfilled d shell and can quench the fluorescence through electron or energy transfer [47]. Then, the presence of the caffeine as analyte induce dissociation of Cu2+ ions from S-CQDs surface, resulting in the Off–On” fluorescence switching. In the previous studies it has been indicated that Cu (II) has a high affinity to caffeine and the interaction between caffeine and Cu2+ ions occurs because of oxygen and nitrogen atoms of the caffeine molecule [48,49]. To further study the sensing process, the Zeta potential of S-CQDs were studied. S-CQDs alone have a Zeta potential of about −24.1 mV, while a decrease of Zeta potential of −11.9 mV was observed in the presence of Cu 2+. With the addition of Caffeine, an increase in zeta potential was observed, and this can be attributed to the reaction of the Cu 2+ cations with Caffeine, which effectively removes the Cu 2+ ions from the surface of the S-CQDs (see Fig. S8).

Under the optimal experimental conditions, label-free and ultrasensitive sensing of caffeine could be easily realized. As evident from Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of S-CQD- Cu 2+ was gradually restored as caffeine concentration increased. A linear relationship between the fluorescence enhancement factor F0–F/F0 (F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensities of S-CQD- Cu 2+ at 435 nm in the presence and absence of Caffeine, respectively) and Caffeine concentrations was achieved in the range of 0.2 μM–70 μM (Fig. 6B), with a linear regression equation of Y = 0.0657 [Caffeine] (μM)+0.0602, R2 = 0.994. The limit of detection for Caffeine was calculated to be 0.05 μM according to 3Sb/m. Table 1
lists the comparison between the proposed fluorescence probe and other previously reported methods for caffeine detection. Obviously, our proposed caffeine sensing strategy displayed analytical parameters comparable to other methods. Furthermore, this approach offers advantages such as low cost preparation, simple instrumentation, and rapid testing procedure.

To evaluate the specificity of the S-CQDs- Cu2+ system for caffeine detection, the fluorescence responses to the other interfering substances were also evaluated under the same conditions. Upon adding caffeine to the solution of S-CQDs- Cu2+, the quenched fluorescence of S-CQDs was recovered, whereas slight fluorescence enhancement of the probe was observed after treatment with 300 μM of ascorbic acid, glucose, fructose,citric acid and theophylline and 1000 μM Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Mn2+ and Mg2+ (Fig. 7 A). In addition, to evaluate the anti-interference property of the present detection system toward caffeine, we performed the competitive experiments in the presence of interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 7B, in the presence of interferences, no significant change in the fluorescence signal of probe towards caffeine occurred. The reduction of the Cu2+ to Cu+ is relatively slow, thus a reductive substance such as ascorbic acid did not interfere with the detection of caffeine [50]. The excellent selectivity combined with fast response of S-CQDs to caffeine suggests that this method might be used as a selective and sensitive system for caffeine detection in real samples.

The applicability of the sensing system was assessed in the detection of caffeine in some real samples. The samples were first diluted to bring the concentration within the range of detection limits. Recovery studies were performed by standard addition method, and the values were determined according to the following equation: recovery (%) = 100 × (observed amount − original amount)/spiked amount. Table 2 shows the analytical results for the analyzed samples. The relative recovery values of 94.6–103.8% were obtained from spiked samples with three repeated measurements. The results demonstrate the applicability of the procedure for the detection of caffeine in real samples.

The aim of the present research was to develop a simple, sensitive and cost-efficient method for detection of trace amounts of caffeine in food samples. The obtained S-CQDs were shown to be a novel fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive and selective detecting caffeine.The effect of operating parameters on the fluorescence sensing of caffeine were mathematically measured by central composite design (CCD) under response surface methodology (RSM). The linear response range between 0.2 and 70 μM and the limit of detection 0.05 μM for caffeine was achieved. Importantly, this fluorescent probe was capable of successfully detecting caffeine in real samples with satisfactory results. In conclusion, the proposed S-CQDs −based sensing strategy provided a rapid and highly efficient assay for the detection of caffeine, which can be considered as a promising candidate for the analysis of caffeine in food industry.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the research Council of University of Tehran (Grant 28645/01/02) and Iran University of Science and Technology for financial support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.05.163.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polymer modified glassy carbon electrode for the electrochemical determination of caffeine in coffee
Talanta
Simultaneous determination of caffeine and paracetamol by square wave voltammetry at poly(4-amino-3-hydroxynaphthalene sulfonic acid)-modified glassy carbon electrode
Food Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Fatemeh Nemati is a Ph.D. student in analytical chemistry in University of Science and Technology.

Morteza Hosseini has a Ph.D. in analytical chemistry obtained at Tarbiat Modares University in 2005. He is currently an associated professor in nanobiotechnology group of Faculty of New Sciences and Technologies of University of Tehran. His research work has been mainly focused on the design of new optical nanosensors and nanobiosensors.

Rouholah Zare-Dorabei has a Ph.D. in analytical chemistry obtained at University of Science and Technology. He is currently a assistance professor of analytical chemistry at University of Tehran. His research work has been mainly focused on the design of new chemical sensors.

Mohammad Reza Ganjali has a Ph.D. in analytical chemistry obtained at University of Tehran in 1997. He is currently a professor of analytical chemistry at University of Tehran. His research work has been mainly focused on the design of new chemical sensors.
